does: This paper introduces Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework that trains reward models to evaluate the quality of orchestration decisions in multi-agent LLM systems without requiring human annotations. The method constructs Bradley-Terry reward model training pairs from intermediate artifacts generated during multi-agent execution, enabling the reward model to score orchestration choices directly. OrchRM achieves substantial efficiency gains—up to 10x reduction in token usage during training and up to 8% accuracy improvements during test-time scaling—while eliminating the need for expensive sub-agent rollouts that plague existing orchestration training methods. The framework is validated across diverse reasoning tasks including mathematical problem-solving, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning benchmarks.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on multi-agent system coordination and test-time scaling for LLMs. It builds on prior research in reward modeling, particularly Bradley-Terry models for learning from pairwise comparisons, and connects to recent work on training orchestrators for multi-agent systems. The paper responds to computational bottlenecks identified in existing frameworks that require expensive rollouts from specialized sub-agents, a problem highlighted in work on process reward models and ensemble methods for LLMs. OrchRM advances beyond single-agent scaling approaches by focusing on the orchestration layer itself, building on insights from hierarchical control and delegated reasoning in multi-agent architectures.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more scalable training of multi-agent orchestrators in practical applications where human annotation budgets are severely constrained. It suggests future directions for developing increasingly sophisticated reward modeling approaches at higher levels of multi-agent abstractions, potentially enabling larger and more specialized agent teams without proportional increases in training costs. The self-supervised construction of training pairs from execution traces could extend to other aspects of multi-agent coordination beyond orchestration, such as inter-agent communication and dynamic team composition. The efficiency gains suggest that orchestration-level reward guidance could become a standard component of production multi-agent systems, paving the way for more ambitious applications in planning, scientific discovery, and complex decision-making.
